documentation:
# pcdscalc

```{image} https://img.shields.io/travis/pcdshub/pcdscalc.svg
:target: https://travis-ci.org/pcdshub/pcdscalc
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/pcdscalc.svg
:target: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pcdscalc
```

PCDS Control-system Agnostic Calculation Routines

## Documentation

<https://pcdshub.github.io/pcdscalc/>

## Requirements

- Python 3.6+
- See [requirements].

## Installation

```
$ pip install git+https://github.com/pcdshub/pcdscalc
```

## Running the Tests

```
$ pytest -vv
```

[requirements]: requirements.txt

functions.name: pcdscalc.tests.test_pmps.test_get_bitmask
documentation:
Test that the correct bitmask is created.

Explanation of test cases 3 to 8 (first two are obvious):
3. Allow between 0 and 15, exclude the 15.5 point because we can't allow
   points like 15.6 and they share a range. Therefore, enable bits 1
   through 15 (bit 1 allows 0 to 1, bit 15 allows 14 to 15).
4. Allow between 16 and 32, exclude the 15.5 point because we can't allow
   points like 15.4 and they share a range. Cut off at 32 for the top of
   the bitmask range. Therefore, enable bits 17 through 32 (bit 17 allows
   16 to 17).
5. Allow between 15 and 21, exclude the exact points because we can't allow
   any points outside the range. Enable bits 16 to 21 (bit 16 allows 15 to
   16, bit 21 allows 20 to 21).
6. Allow between 0 and 14 and between 22 and 32. Turn off bits 15 to 22
   (bit 15 allows 14 to 15, bit 22 allows 21 to 22). Exclude the boundary
   bits for the same reasonings as above.
7. Allow exactly between 15 and 20. Turn on bits 16 to 20 (bit 16 allows 15
   to 16, bit 20 allows 19 to 20)
8. Turn off bits 16 to 20 (bit 16 allows 15 to 16, bit 20 allows 19 to 20)

modules.name: pcdscalc.constants
documentation: "Module to hold constants.

modules.name: pcdscalc.common
documentation: Module that holds common calculations.

functions.name: pcdscalc.common.get_energy
documentation:
Returns energy in eV.

Parameters
----------
energy: float
    energy in eV or keV

functions.name: pcdscalc.common.cosd
documentation:
Cos of an angle specified in degrees.

Parameters
----------
angle : number
    Angle in degrees.

Returns
-------
arad : number
    The corresponding cosine value of `angle` input.

functions.name: pcdscalc.common.sind
documentation:
Sin of an angle specified in degrees.

Parameters
----------
angle : number
    Angle in degrees.

Returns
-------
arad : number
    The corresponding sine value of `angle` input.

functions.name: pcdscalc.common.tand
documentation:
Tan of an angle specified in degrees.

Parameters
----------
angle : number
    Angle in degrees.

Returns
-------
arad : number
    The corresponding tangent value of `angle` input.

functions.name: pcdscalc.common.asind
documentation:
Arcsin in degrees. Closed interval of [-pi/2, pi/2].

Parameters
----------
x : number
    y-coordinate on the unit circle.

Returns
-------
arad : number
    The inverse sine of x in degrees.

functions.name: pcdscalc.common.acosd
documentation:
Arccos in degrees.

Parameters
----------
x : number
    x-coordinate on the unit circle.
    For real arguments the domain is [-1, 1].

Returns
-------
arad : number
    The angle of the ray intersecting the unit circle at the given
    x-coordinate in degrees.

functions.name: pcdscalc.common.atand
documentation:
Arctan in degrees.

Parameters
----------
x : number

Returns
-------
arad : number
    Has the same shape as x. Its real part is in [-pi/2, pi/2]
    (arctan(+/-inf) returns +/-pi/2).

functions.name: pcdscalc.common.energy_to_wavelength
documentation:
Compute photon wavelength in m.

Parameters

Returns

functions.name: pcdscalc.common.wavelength_to_energy
documentation:
Compute photon energy in eV.

Parameters

Returns
-------

classes.name: pcdscalc.version.VersionProxy
documentation:
Version handling helper that pairs with setuptools-scm.

This allows for pkg.__version__ to be dynamically retrieved on request by
way of setuptools-scm.

This deferred evaluation of the version until it is checked saves time on
package import.

This supports the following scenarios:

1. A git checkout (.git exists)
2. A git archive / a tarball release from GitHub that includes version
    information in .git_archival.txt.
3. An existing _version.py generated by setuptools_scm
4. A fallback in case none of the above match - resulting in a version of
    0.0.unknown

modules.name: pcdscalc._version
documentation: file generated by setuptools_scm
don't change, don't track in version control

functions.name: pcdscalc.xray.molecular_mass

Returns the total photoabsorption cross section in m^2
NOTE: This is per molecule if chemical formula given

Parameters
----------

energy: float

functions.name: pcdscalc.xray.cs_compt
documentation:
Returns the total Compton (inelastic) cross section in m^2

Parameters
----------
material_id: str
    Chemical fomula : 'Si'
energy: float

functions.name: pcdscalc.xray.attenuation_length
documentation:
Computes the attenuation length of a solid in m (photoabsoption and Compton cross sections)

Parameters
----------
material_id: str
    Chemical fomula : 'Si'
energy: float
    E is photon energy in eV
density: float
    Material density in g/cm^3
    If no density is specified will use default value

functions.name: pcdscalc.xray.transmission
documentation:
Computes the transmission of a solid (photoabsoption and Compton cross sections)

Parameters
----------

    Chemical fomula : 'Si'
thickness: float
    Material thickness in m
energy: float
    E is photon energy in eV or keV
density: float
    Material density in g/cm^3
    If no density is specified will use default value

modules.name: pcdscalc.diffraction
documentation:
Module that handles calculations for diffraction/crystallography.

Assume all the calculations that expect energy as input will
require energy in eV.

functions.name: pcdscalc.diffraction.get_lom_geometry
documentation:
Calculate the Large Offset Monochromator's crystal geometry.

Parameters
----------
energy : number
    Photon energy in eV.
material_id : str
    Chemical formula. E.g.: `Si`.
reflection : tuple
    The reflection. E.g.: `(1,1,1)`.

Returns
-------
thm, zm : tuple

functions.name: pcdscalc.diffraction.bragg_angle
documentation:
Compute the Bragg angle in deg.

Computes the bragg angle of the specified material, reflection and photon
energy.

Parameters
----------
material_id : str
    Chemical formula. Defaults to `Si`.
hkl : tuple
    The reflection indices. Defaults to `(1,1,1)`.
energy : number
    The photon energy in eV.

Returns
-------
theta : number
    Theta in degrees.

functions.name: pcdscalc.diffraction.d_space
documentation:
Compute the d spacing (m) of the specified material and reflection.

The d-spacing can be described as the distance between planes of atoms
that give rise to diffraction peaks. Each peak in a diffractogram results
from a corresponding d-spacing. The planes of atoms can be referred to a
3D coordinate system and so can be described as a direction within the
crystal. So d-spacing as well as having a dimension, usually quoted in
Ångstroms, can be labelled with a plane direction hkl.

Parameters
----------
material_id : str
    Chemical fomula. E.g.: `Si`
hkl : tuple
    Miller Indices, the reflection. E.g.: `(1,1,1)`

Returns
-------
d : number
    The lattice plane spacing d in Bragg's Law for the chosen plane
    indicated by the Miller indices.

functions.name: pcdscalc.diffraction.form_factor
documentation:
Returns the atomic form factor for Rayleigh scattering

Parameters
----------
material_id: str
twotheta: float
    scattering angle in degrees
energy: float

functions.name: pcdscalc.diffraction.unit_cell_volume
documentation: Unit cell volume

functions.name: pcdscalc.diffraction.darwin_width
documentation:
Computes the Darwin width for a specified crystal reflection (degrees)

Parameters
----------
material_id : str
    Chemical fomula. E.g.: `Si`
hkl : tuple
    Miller Indices, the reflection. E.g.: `(1,1,1)`
E : float
    Photon energy in eV
T : float
    Temperature in Kelvin.

Returns
-------
darwin_width : float
    Darwin width in deg of the reflection

modules.name: pcdscalc.pmps
documentation:
Calculation utilities related to the LCLS PMPS system.

Here you will find functions related to eV bitmasks.

functions.name: pcdscalc.pmps.select_bitmask_boundaries
documentation:
Given a line, select the bitmask boundaries to use.

These are hard-coded for now but in theory this could be loaded from
a database, an EPICS PV, or some other source.

Parameters
----------
line: str
    String representation of which line's bitmask to use.
    If the string begins with "l" or "h" (lfe, hxr), we'll
    use the hard-xray bitmask.
    If the string begins with "k" or "s" (kfe, sxr), we'll
    use the soft-xray bitmask.

Returns
-------
boundaries: list of floats

functions.name: pcdscalc.pmps.get_bitmask
documentation:
Given a range of eV values, calculate the appropriate pmps bitmask.

This saves you the effort of checking up on the eV ranges and
remembering how the bitmask is assembled.

If the lower or upper fall within a range (not on the border), then that
range is always considered unsafe.

The rules for the bitmasks are:
    - The nth bit of the bitmask represents a range from the n-1th value
      to the nth value
    - If the bit is 1, the range is allowed and is safe.
    - If the bit is 0, the range is not allowed and is not safe.
    - eVs above the highest boundary are never safe
    - negative eVs are never safe

Some examples:
    - bitmask 00 -> no beam range is safe
    - bitmask 01 -> only the lowest boundary and below are safe
    - bitmask 11 -> only the two lowest boundaries and below are safe
    - bitmask 10 -> only the space between the lowest and second-lowest
                    boundaries are safe.
    - bitmask all 1s -> every eV is OK except for above the highest
                        boundary and except for negative eV

Parameters
----------
lower: number
    The value in eV for the lower bound of the range.

upper: number
    The value in eV for the upper bound of the range.

allow: bool
    True if we want a bitmask that only includes this range,
    False if we want a bitmask that only excludes this range.

line: str
    String representation of which line's bitmask to use.
    If the string begins with "l" or "h" (lfe, hxr), we'll
    use the hard-xray bitmask.
    If the string begins with "k" or "s" (kfe, sxr), we'll
    use the soft-xray bitmask.

bounds: list of numbers, optional
    Custom boundaries to use instead of the default soft-xray
    or hard-xray lines. Useful for testing.

Returns
-------
bitmask: int

functions.name: pcdscalc.pmps.check_bitmask
documentation:
Given an energy and a bitmask, tell us if our energy is allowed.

This is the same calculation the PMPS is doing internally to determine
if it is safe for beam to proceed.

Parameters
----------
energy: number
    The value in eV for the energy to check.

bitmask: int
    The bits to check against. Typically an output of `get_bitmask`.

line: str
    String representation of which line's bitmask to use.
    If the string begins with "l" or "h" (lfe, hxr), we'll
    use the hard-xray bitmask.
    If the string begins with "k" or "s" (kfe, sxr), we'll
    use the soft-xray bitmask.

bounds: list of numbers, optional
    Custom boundaries to use instead of the default soft-xray
    or hard-xray lines. Useful for testing.

Returns
-------
energy_allowed: bool
    True if the energy is allowed.

functions.name: pcdscalc.pmps.check_actual_range
documentation:
Returns the actual effective range given bitmask precision.

Because of the granularity of the bitmask, most range specifications
exclude more energy values than requested.

Parameters
----------
lower: number
    The value in eV for the lower bound of the range.

upper: number
    The value in eV for the upper bound of the range.

allow: bool
    True if we want a bitmask that only includes this range,
    False if we want a bitmask that only excludes this range.

line: str
    String representation of which line's bitmask to use.
    If the string begins with "l" or "h" (lfe, hxr), we'll
    use the hard-xray bitmask.
    If the string begins with "k" or "s" (kfe, sxr), we'll
    use the soft-xray bitmask.

bounds: list of numbers, optional
    Custom boundaries to use instead of the default soft-xray
    or hard-xray lines. Useful for testing.

Returns
-------
ranges: tuple
    A (lower, upper) pair that represents a range of allowed
    (or forbidden) energy values. The endpoints of the range
    are considered unsafe.

functions.name: pcdscalc.pmps.describe_bitmask
documentation:
Print a text description of a bitmask.

This will describe what the bitmask means.

Parameters
----------
bitmask : int
    The bits to describe. Typically an output of `get_bitmask`.

line : str
    String representation of which line's bitmask to use.
    If the string begins with "l" or "h" (lfe, hxr), we'll
    use the hard-xray bitmask.
    If the string begins with "k" or "s" (kfe, sxr), we'll
    use the soft-xray bitmask.

bounds : list of numbers, optional
    Custom boundaries to use instead of the default soft-xray
    or hard-xray lines. Useful for testing.

functions.name: pcdscalc.pmps.get_bitmask_desc
documentation:
Return a text description of a bitmask.

This will describe what the bitmask means.

Parameters
----------
bitmask : int
    The bits to describe. Typically an output of `get_bitmask`.

line : str
    String representation of which line's bitmask to use.
    If the string begins with "l" or "h" (lfe, hxr), we'll
    use the hard-xray bitmask.
    If the string begins with "k" or "s" (kfe, sxr), we'll
    use the soft-xray bitmask.

bounds : list of numbers, optional
    Custom boundaries to use instead of the default soft-xray
    or hard-xray lines. Useful for testing.

Returns
-------
desc : list of str
    Description lines that describe what the bitmask means.

modules.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs
documentation: Module for Beryllium Lens Calculations.

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.configure_defaults
documentation:
Configure defaults.

# Parameters

fwhm_unfocused

: Full width at half maximum unfocused in meters.

disk_thickness

: Disk thickness in meters.

apex_distance

: Apex Distance in meters.

distance

: Distance from the lenses to the sample in meters.

material

: Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

lens_radii

: Set of Be lenses with thicknesses in meters.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> configure_defaults(distance=3.852)
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.configure_lens_set_file
documentation:
Configure the path to the lens set file.

Parameters
----------
lens_file_path : str
    Path to the lens_set file.

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.photon_to_wavelength
documentation:
Find the wavelength in micrometers.

Use photon energy in electronvolts. The equation is approximately: λ[µm] =
1.2398 / E[eV]. The photon energy at 1 μm wavelength, the wavelength of
near infrared radiation, is approximately 1.2398 eV.

# Parameters

energy

: Photon energy in electronvolts

# Returns

Wavelength in micrometers

# Examples

```pycon
>>> photon_to_wavelength(8)
0.154975
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.gaussian_sigma_to_fwhm
documentation:
Convert between FWHM and sigma of a Gaussian function.

FWHM = 2.35482004503 * sigma

<https://brainder.org/2011/08/20/gaussian-kernels-convert-fwhm-to-sigma/>

# Parameters

sigma : float

# Returns

FWHM - Full Width at the Half Maximum

# Examples

```pycon
>>> gaussian_sigma_to_fwhm(0.3)
0.7064460135089999
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.gaussian_fwhm_to_sigma
documentation:
Convert between FWHM and sigma of a Gaussian function.

sigma = FWHM / 2.35482004503

<https://brainder.org/2011/08/20/gaussian-kernels-convert-fwhm-to-sigma/>

# Parameters

fwhm

: Full Width at the Half Maximum.

# Returns

sigma

# Examples

```pycon
>>> gaussian_fwhm_to_sigma(0.023)
0.009767200703316157
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.get_lens_set
documentation:
Get the lens set from the file provided.

Parameters
----------
set_number_top_to_bot : int
    The Be lens holders can take 3 different sets that we usually set
    before experiments, this is to specify what number set.
filename : str, optional
    File path of the lens_set file.
get_all : bool
    Indicates if it should return the entire stack of lens.

Returns
-------
lens_set : list
    [numer1, lensthick1, number2, lensthick2 ...]

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.set_lens_set_to_file
documentation:
Write lens set to a file.

The Be lens holders can take 3 different sets that we usually set before
few experiments so we only vent the relevant beamline section once. We then
store these sets into a config file that we can call from some methods.
Later, we save this config file to the specific experiment so users can
make sure that they know which stack was used for there beamtime.

# Parameters

list_of_sets

: List of lists with lens sets.

filename

: Path to the filename to set the lens sets list to.
  This should be a .npy format file.

make_backup

: To indicate if a backup file should be created or not.
  Defaults to `True`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> list_of_sets = [[3, 0.0001, 1, 0.0002],
                    [1, 0.0001, 1, 0.0003, 1, 0.0005],
                    [2, 0.0001, 1, 0.0005]]
>>> set_lens_set_to_file(list_of_sets, '../path/to/lens_set')
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.get_att_len
documentation:
Get the attenuation length (in meter) of a material.

The X-ray beam intensity I(x) at depth x in a material is a function of the
attenuation coefficient mu, and can be calculated by the Beer-Lambert law.
The Absorption Length (or Attenuation Length) is defined as the distance
into a material where the x-ray beam intensity has decreased to a value of
1/e (~ 40%) of the incident beam intensity (Io).

(1/e) = e^(-mu * x)
ln(1/e) = ln(e^(-mu * x))
1 = mu * x
x = 1/mu

# Parameters

energy

: Beam Energy given in KeV

material

: Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

# Returns

att_len

: Attenuation length (in meters)

# Raises

ValueError

: If an invalid symbol is provided for material.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> get_att_len(energy=8, material='Be')
0.004810113254120656
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.get_delta
documentation:
Calculate delta for a given material at a given energy.

Anomalous components of the index of refraction for a material, using the
tabulated scattering components from Chantler.

# Parameters

energy

: x-ray energy in KeV

material

: Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

# Returns

delta

: Real part of index of refraction

# Raises

ValueError

: If an invalid symbol is provided for material.

ZeroDivisionError

: When energy is 0.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> get_delta(energy=8, material='Au')
4.728879989419882e-05
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.calc_focal_length_for_single_lens
documentation:
Calculate the Focal Length for a single lens.

# Parameters

energy

: Beam Energy in in KeV

radius

: Radius of curvature (in meters)

material

: Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

# Returns

focal_length

: The focal length for a single lens

# Examples

```pycon
>>> calc_focal_length_for_single_lens(8, 0.03, 'Be')
2814.0101061895903
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.calc_focal_length
documentation:
Calculate the Focal Length for certain lenses configuration and energy.

# Parameters

energy

: Beam Energy in KeV

lens_set

: [numer1, lensthick1, number2, lensthick2 ...]

material

: Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

# Returns

focal_length : float

# Examples

```pycon
>>> calc_focal_length(8, [1, 0.3, 2, 0.4], 'Be')
11256.040424758363
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.calc_beam_fwhm
documentation:
Calculate beam Full Width at the Half Maximum.

Calculate beam parameters for certain lenses configuration and energy at a
given distance. FWHM - Full Width at the Half Maximum Optionally some
other parameters can be set.

# Parameters

energy

: Beam Energy in KeV

lens_set

: [numer1, lensthick1, number2, lensthick2...]

distance

: Distance from the lenses to the sample is 3.852 m at XPP. (in meters)

source_distance

: Distance from source to lenses. This is about 160 m at XPP. (in meters)

prefocus_set: list, optional

: [numer1, lensthick1, number2, lensthick2...]

prefocus_distance: float

: distance from prefocusing lenses to hutch lenses. 27 m for XCS.

material

: Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

fwhm_unfocused

: This is about 400 microns at XPP.
  This is about 900 microns at MEC.
  Radial size of x-ray beam before focusing in meters.

printsummary

: Prints summary of parameters/calculations if `True`.

# Returns

size_fwhm

: Beam Full Width at the Half Maximum in meters.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> calc_beam_fwhm(energy=9, lens_set=[2, 0.03, 4, 0.002], distance=4,
                   material='Be', fwhm_unfocused=800e-6)
0.0008373516816325981
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.calc_distance_for_size
documentation:
Calculate the distance for size.

# Parameters

size_fwhm

: Beam Full Width at the Half Maximum in meters.

lens_set

: [numer1, lensthick1, number2, lensthick2...]

energy

: Beam Energy in KeV

fwhm_unfocused

: This is about 400 microns at XPP.
  Radial size of x-ray beam before focusing in meters.

# Returns

distance

: Distance in meters

# Examples

```pycon
>>> calc_distance_for_size(0.023, [2, 0.03, 4, 0.002], 8, 0.078)
array([32.00383702, 58.77068253])
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.calc_lens_aperture_radius
documentation:
Calculate the lens aperture radius.

It is of importance to optimize which lens radius to use at a specific
photon energy.

# Parameters

radius : float
disk_thickness : float, optional

> Disk Thickness in meters. Defaults to 1.0e-3.

apex_distance

: Apex Distance in meters. Defaults to 30e-6

# Returns

aperture_radius : float

# Examples

```pycon
>>> calc_lens_aperture_radius(radius=4.0, disk_thickness=1e-3,
                              apex_distance=30e-6)
0.06228964600958975
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.calc_trans_for_single_lens
documentation:
Calculate the transmission for a single lens.

# Parameters

energy

: Beam Energy in KeV

radius : float
material : str, optional

> Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

fwhm_unfocused

: This is about 400 microns at XPP.
  Radial size of x-ray beam before focusing in meters.

disk_thickness

: Disk Thickness in meters. Defaults to 1.0e-3.

apex_distance

: Apex Distance in meters. Defaults to 30e-6.

# Returns

transmission

: Transmission for a single lens

# Examples

```pycon
>>> calc_trans_for_single_lens(energy=8, radius=0.03, material='Be',
                               density=None, fwhm_unfocused=800e-6,
                               disk_thickness=1.0e-3, apex_distance=30e-6)
0.9921954096643786
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.calc_trans_lens_set
documentation:
Calculate the transmission of a lens set.

These would allow us to estimate the total transmission of the lenses.

# Parameters

energy

: Beam Energy in KeV

lens_set

: [numer1, lensthick1, number2, lensthick2...]

material

: Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

fwhm_unfocused

: This is about 400 microns at XPP. Default = 900e-6
  Radial size of x-ray beam before focusing in meters.

disk_thickness

: Disk Thickness in meters. Defaults to 1.0e-3.

apex_distance

: Apex Distance in meters. Defaults to 30e-6.

# Returns

transmission

: Transmission for a set of lens

# Examples

```pycon
>>> calc_trans_lens_set(energy=8, lens_set=[1, 0.03, 4, 0.02],
                        material='Be', density=None,
                        fwhm_unfocused=400e-6)
0.955752311215339
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.calc_lens_set
documentation:
Calculate lens set.

# Parameters

energy

: Beam Energy in KeV

size_fwhm

: Beam Full Width at the Half Maximum. (in meters)

distance

: Distance from the lenses to the sample. (in meters)

n_max

: Number of lenses.

max_each : int, optional
lens_radii : list, optional

> [numer1, lensthick1, number2, lensthick2...]

fwhm_unfocused

: This is about 400 microns at XPP. Defaults to 500e-6.
  Radial size of x-ray beam before focusing in meters.

eff_rad0 : float, optional

# Returns

lens_sets

: Lens sets

# Examples

```pycon
>>> calc_lens_set(energy=7, size_fwhm=0.54, distance=3)
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.find_radius
documentation:
Find the radius of curvature.

Find the radius of curvature of the lens that would focus the energy at the
distance.

# Parameters

energy

: Beam Energy

distance : float, optional
material : str, optional

> Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

# Returns

radius

: Radius of curvature in meters.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> find_radius(energy=8, distance=4.0, material='Be', density=None)
4.2643770090253954e-05
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.find_energy
documentation:
Find the energy that would focus at a given distance.

# Parameters

lens_set

: [numer1, lensthick1, number2, lensthick2...]

distance : float, optional
material : str, optional

> Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

# Returns

energy

: Energy

# Examples

```pycon
>>> find_energy([2, 200e-6, 4, 500e-6], distance=4)
7.0100555419921875
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.find_z_pos
documentation:
Find the Be Lens distances.

Find the two distances the Be lens needs to be at to get the spotsize in
the chamber center.

# Parameters

energy

: Beam Energy in KeV

lens_set

: [numer1, lensthick1, number2, lensthick2...]

spot_size_fwhm :
material : str, optional

> Atomic symbol for element, defaults to 'Be'.

density

: Material density in g/cm^3

fwhm_unfocused

: This is about 400 microns at XPP. Defaults to 500e-6
  Radial size of x-ray beam before focusing in meters.

# Returns

z_position

: (z1, z2)

# Examples

```pycon
>>> lens_set = [2, 200e-6, 4, 500e-6]
>>> find_z_pos(energy=8, lens_set=lens_set, spot_size_fwhm=0.09,
               material='Be', density=None, fwhm_unfocused=800e-6)
(-2339.797291538794, 2350.2195511913483)
```

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.plan_set
documentation:
Macro to help plan for what lens set to use.

# Parameters

energy

: Beam energy in KeV

z_offset

: Distance from sample to lens_z=0 in meters

z_range

: Array or tuple of 2 values: minimum and maximum z pos in meters

beam_size_unfocused

: Radial size of x-ray beam before focusing in meters

size_horizontal : array-like
size_vertical : None, int or tuple of ints, optional
exclude : list

> List with excluded sets of lenses

max_tot_number_of_lenses

: Number of lenses.

max_each : int
focus_before_sample : bool

# Examples

```pycon
>>> plan_set(energy=1, z_offset=-10, z_range=[1, 40],
                       beam_size_unfocused=3, size_horizontal=9,
                       size_vertical=None, exclude=[],
                       max_tot_number_of_lenses=1,
                       max_each=5, focus_before_sample=False)
```

N foc_len/m Min/um Max/um Trans/% Set
0 0.07 466994229.0 2112064677.4 0.0 1 x 50um
1 0.14 234997114.5 1057532338.7 0.0 1 x 100um
2 0.28 118998557.3 530266169.4 0.0 1 x 200um
3 0.43 80332371.5 354510779.6 0.0 1 x 300um
4 0.71 49399422.9 213906467.7 0.0 1 x 500um
5 1.42 26199711.5 108453233.9 0.0 1 x 1000um
6 2.13 18466474.3 73302155.9 0.0 1 x 1500um
7 2.84 14599855.7 55726616.9 0.0 1 x 2000um
8 4.27 10733237.2 38151078.0 0.0 1 x 3000um

Where:
`foc_len` - is the focal length in um
`Min` - Beam full width at half maximum for minimum z position
`Max` - Beam full width at half maximum for maximum z position
`Trans` - is the lens transmission in %.

# Notes

When providing a large number to `max_tot_number_of_lenses`,
it will take some time to run the calculations, for example:
`max_tot_number_of_lenses=25` will take ~ 2 min

functions.name: pcdscalc.be_lens_calcs.lens_transmission
documentation:
Find the CRL (Compound Refractive Lens) transmission.

Parameters
----------
radius : float
    Effective radius of curvature in meters
fwhm : float
    Incident beam size on the lens in meters
num : int
    Number of lenses in the stack
energy : number
    Photon energy in KeV
id_material : str
    Lens material, Defaults to `IF1`
lens_thickness : float
    Lens thickness in meters

Returns
-------
trans : float
    Lens Transmission
